data.title: Cold Exposure and Systemic Inflammation
data.claim: Repeated cold immersion lowers circulating inflammatory markers
data.summary: A 6-week immersion protocol reduced IL-6 and CRP versus controls, with the effect concentrated in participants who completed at least 80 percent of sessions. The trial was small and unblinded, so we read it as supportive, not settled.

data.title: Cold Water and Mood Regulation
data.claim: Cold immersion produces a measurable short-term lift in mood and alertness
data.summary: Self-reported mood and vigor rose for two to four hours after immersion, with a noradrenaline surge as the likely mechanism. Durable mood effects beyond a single day were not demonstrated, and we say so on the intake call.

data.title: Habituation of the Cold Shock Response
data.claim: Repeated short exposures blunt the initial cold shock response
data.summary: After six immersions the gasp reflex and the spike in heart rate and ventilation fell substantially. This is the most robust finding in the field and underpins our short, supervised early sessions.

data.title: Cold Immersion and Heart-Rate Variability
data.claim: Regular cold exposure is associated with a modest rise in resting HRV
data.summary: HRV trended upward over eight weeks in regular immersers, but the cohort was self-selected and unblinded. We track HRV per client precisely because the group-level signal is weak and individual response varies.

data.title: Brown Adipose Tissue Activation
data.claim: Cold exposure activates brown fat and raises energy expenditure short-term
data.summary: Cold reliably activated brown adipose tissue and modestly increased energy expenditure during exposure. Claims that this drives meaningful weight loss are not supported by the data, and we do not make them.

data.title: Cold Exposure and Sleep Quality
data.claim: Evening cold exposure may shorten sleep onset in some people
data.summary: A small pilot found shorter reported sleep onset after late-afternoon immersion, with no change in total sleep time. The effect was inconsistent across participants and the sample was tiny.

data.title: Delayed-Onset Muscle Soreness After Cold
data.claim: Cold-water immersion reduces perceived muscle soreness after exercise
data.summary: Pooled trials show a reliable reduction in perceived soreness 24 to 72 hours post-exercise. Effects on actual performance recovery are smaller and noisier, and chronic use may blunt training adaptation.

data.title: Cold Immersion and Immune Markers
data.claim: Routine cold exposure correlates with fewer self-reported sick days
data.summary: A large pragmatic trial found fewer self-reported sick-leave days in the cold-shower group, though sickness severity was unchanged. Self-report and lack of blinding temper the conclusion.

data.title: Norepinephrine Response to Cold
data.claim: Cold immersion produces a large acute rise in plasma norepinephrine
data.summary: Immersion produced a two-to-three-fold rise in norepinephrine, a plausible mechanism for the acute mood and alertness lift. Whether this translates to durable clinical benefit is a separate, weaker question.

data.title: Cardiovascular Load During Cold Immersion
data.claim: Sudden cold immersion sharply raises cardiac workload
data.summary: Immersion produced immediate rises in heart rate, blood pressure, and cardiac output. This is exactly why we screen for cardiac risk first and supervise every session; the load is real and not trivial.

data.title: Cold Adaptation and Perceived Stress
data.claim: An adaptation program is associated with lower perceived stress over weeks
data.summary: Perceived stress scores fell modestly across an eight-week program, but with no control group the result cannot be separated from routine, expectation, and attention effects. We present it as a hypothesis, not a claim.

data.title: Chronic Cold and Inflammatory Cytokines
data.claim: Sustained cold practice may shift baseline cytokine profiles
data.summary: Over twelve weeks, several inflammatory cytokines trended lower in adherent participants. The change was small, variable, and of uncertain clinical meaning; we report individual markers rather than promising an outcome.
